query: I'm building a JSON API response for a Netflix-style catalog where I need to return all globally available TV shows with their seasons nested as children. Each TV show should have a `children` array containing all its seasons ordered by season number. My query is returning the same complete list of ALL seasons for every single TV show instead of just the seasons that belong to that specific show. So if there are 50 total seasons across all shows, every TV show in my result set is getting all 50 seasons in its children array, regardless of the `tv_show_id` foreign key. I thought the subquery would automatically filter to the current row's TV show, but it's clearly not doing that. The JSON structure is correct, but the data is completely wrong because there's no relationship being enforced between the outer TV show and the seasons being aggregated in the subquery. How do I make the subquery aware of which TV show it's currently processing so it only includes seasons for that specific show?
issue_sql: SELECT 'tv:' || t.id AS key, t.title AS label, (SELECT json_group_array(json_object('key', key, 'label', label)) FROM (SELECT 'season:' || s.id AS key, s.title AS label FROM season s ORDER BY s.season_number)) AS children FROM tv_show t WHERE t.available_globally = 1 ORDER BY t.id
sol_sql: SELECT 'tv:' || t.id AS key, t.title AS label, (SELECT json_group_array(json_object('key', key, 'label', label)) FROM (SELECT 'season:' || s.id AS key, s.title AS label FROM season s WHERE s.tv_show_id = t.id ORDER BY s.season_number)) AS children FROM tv_show t WHERE t.available_globally = 1 ORDER BY t.id

    # The correct SQL returns different children for each TV show

    second_children = pred_query_result[1][2]

        f"All TV shows have identical children - subquery scope error detected. First: {first_children}, Second: {second_children}"
    
    return 1

query: In the context of the bookstore database, we have a scenario where we need to compare sets of related book titles and author names for each comparison record. Specifically, we want to concatenate the related titles (Set A) and related author names (Set B) associated with each record, sort the items within each concatenated string, and then compare the final sorted strings (A vs B) to see if they are the same. This comparison helps identify if the sets of associated titles and authors are equivalent when order is disregarded.
issue_sql: SELECT (COALESCE(related_title_A1, '') || ' ' || COALESCE(related_title_A2, '') || ' ' || COALESCE(related_title_A3, '')) AS A, (COALESCE(related_author_B1, '') || ' ' || COALESCE(related_author_B2, '') || ' ' || COALESCE(related_author_B3, '')) AS B FROM book_comparison
sol_sql: WITH u AS (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY related_title_A1, related_title_A2, related_title_A3, related_author_B1, related_author_B2, related_author_B3) AS rn FROM book_comparison), v AS (SELECT related_title_A1 AS A, related_author_B1 AS B, rn FROM u UNION ALL SELECT related_title_A2 AS A, related_author_B2 AS B, rn FROM u UNION ALL SELECT related_title_A3 AS A, related_author_B3 AS B, rn FROM u) SELECT GROUP_CONCAT(A, ' ') AS A, GROUP_CONCAT(B, ' ') AS B FROM v GROUP BY rn
preprocess_sql: CREATE TABLE book_comparison (comparison_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, related_title_A1 TEXT, related_title_A2 TEXT, related_title_A3 TEXT, related_author_B1 TEXT, related_author_B2 TEXT, related_author_B3 TEXT)

INSERT INTO book_comparison (comparison_id, related_title_A1, related_title_A2, related_title_A3, related_author_B1, related_author_B2, related_author_B3) VALUES (1, 'The Great Gatsby', 'Moby Dick', 'War and Peace', 'War and Peace', 'The Great Gatsby', 'Moby Dick'), (2, 'The Great Gatsby', NULL, 'War and Peace', 'War and Peace', 'The Great Gatsby', NULL)

My query joins the season table with itself - the first instance gets all season details, and the second calculates the maximum runtime per show. But when I run it, I'm getting way more rows than expected - like hundreds of results when we only have maybe 50-60 TV shows total. issue_sql:
SELECT s1.id, s1.runtime, s1.tv_show_id
FROM season AS s1
INNER JOIN (
    SELECT tv_show_id, MAX(runtime) as max_runtime
    FROM season
) AS s2 ON s1.runtime = s2.max_runtime
sol_sql:
SELECT id, runtime, tv_show_id
FROM season AS s1
WHERE s1.runtime =
(
    SELECT MAX(s2.runtime)
    FROM season AS s2
    WHERE s2.tv_show_id = s1.tv_show_id
)

Let's map 'h' to an arbitrary first name initial, e.g., 'P'. */ WHERE (fs.first_actor_name IS NULL OR NOT fs.first_actor_name LIKE 'P%') /* Map from WHERE (ms.first_element IS NULL OR ms.first_element != 'h') */ GROUP BY f.film_id, fs.actor_count ORDER BY fs.actor_count DESC /* Map from ORDER BY ms.atom_count */

issue_sql: WITH lose_agg AS (SELECT movie_id AS team_api_id, SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%Y', end_date) AS INTEGER) = 2011 THEN hours_viewed END) AS lose_5, SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%Y', end_date) AS INTEGER) = 2012 THEN hours_viewed END) AS lose_10 FROM view_summary GROUP BY movie_id), profit_agg AS (SELECT season_id AS team_api_id, SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%Y', end_date) AS INTEGER) = 2011 THEN hours_viewed END) AS profit_5, SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%Y', end_date) AS INTEGER) = 2012 THEN hours_viewed END) AS profit_10 FROM view_summary GROUP BY season_id) SELECT l.lose_5, p.profit_5, l.lose_10, p.profit_10, COALESCE(p.team_api_id, l.team_api_id) AS team_api_id FROM lose_agg AS l LEFT JOIN profit_agg AS p ON l.team_api_id = p.team_api_id UNION ALL SELECT l.lose_5, p.profit_5, l.lose_10, p.profit_10, COALESCE(p.team_api_id, l.team_api_id) AS team_api_id FROM profit_agg AS p LEFT JOIN lose_agg AS l ON p.team_api_id = l.team_api_id WHERE l.team_api_id IS NULL ORDER BY team_api_id
sol_sql: WITH movie_summary AS (SELECT movie_id AS id, SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%Y', end_date) AS INTEGER) = 2024 THEN hours_viewed ELSE 0 END) AS hours_viewed_2024 FROM view_summary WHERE movie_id IS NOT NULL GROUP BY movie_id), season_summary AS (SELECT season_id AS id, SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%Y', end_date) AS INTEGER) = 2024 THEN hours_viewed ELSE 0 END) AS hours_viewed_2024 FROM view_summary WHERE season_id IS NOT NULL GROUP BY season_id) SELECT SUM(hours_viewed_2024) AS movie_hours_2024, (SELECT SUM(hours_viewed_2024) FROM season_summary) AS season_hours_2024 FROM movie_summary
test_cases:
def test_case(pred_sqls, sol_sqls, db_path, conn, conditions):
    # SQLite doesn't support FULL OUTER JOIN directly, so we need to modify our approach
    # We'll execute the queries and compare the results differently
    
    # For the solution SQL, we need to simulate a FULL OUTER JOIN with UNION
    if 'FULL OUTER JOIN' in sol_sqls[0]:
        # Replace with SQLite compatible syntax
        modified_sol_sql = """WITH movie_summary AS (
            SELECT movie_id AS id, 
                   SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%Y', end_date) AS INTEGER) = 2024 
                       THEN hours_viewed ELSE 0 END) AS hours_viewed_2024 
            FROM view_summary 
            WHERE movie_id IS NOT NULL 
            GROUP BY movie_id
        ), 
        season_summary AS (
            SELECT season_id AS id, 
                   SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%Y', end_date) AS INTEGER) = 2024 
                       THEN hours_viewed ELSE 0 END) AS hours_viewed_2024 
            FROM view_summary 
            WHERE season_id IS NOT NULL 
            GROUP BY season_id
        )
        SELECT SUM(m.hours_viewed_2024) AS movie_hours_2024, 
               (SELECT SUM(hours_viewed_2024) FROM season_summary) AS season_hours_2024
        FROM movie_summary m"""
        
        # Execute the modified solution SQL
        sol_results = execute_queries([modified_sol_sql], db_path, conn)
        
        # Execute the prediction SQL
        pred_results = execute_queries(pred_sqls, db_path, conn)
        
        # Compare the results
        if pred_results and sol_results:
            # Check if the aggregated values match
            pred_movie_hours = 0
            pred_season_hours = 0
            
            # Extract values from prediction results
            for row in pred_results[0]:
                if row[0] is not None:
                    pred_movie_hours += row[0] if row[0] else 0
                if row[1] is not None:
                    pred_season_hours += row[1] if row[1] else 0
            
            # Extract values from solution results
            sol_movie_hours = sol_results[0][0][0] if sol_results[0][0][0] else 0
            sol_season_hours = sol_results[0][0][1] if sol_results[0][0][1] else 0
            
            # Compare the aggregated values
            if abs(pred_movie_hours - sol_movie_hours) < 0.01 and abs(pred_season_hours - sol_season_hours) < 0.01:
                return 1
            else:
                assert False, f"Results don't match: pred_movie_hours={pred_movie_hours}, sol_movie_hours={sol_movie_hours}, pred_season_hours={pred_season_hours}, sol_season_hours={sol_season_hours}"
        else:
            assert False, "No results returned from one or both queries"
    else:
        # For other cases, use the standard comparison
        pred_sqls = remove_distinct(pred_sqls)
        sol_sqls = remove_distinct(sol_sqls)
        result = ex_base(pred_sqls, sol_sqls, db_path, conn, conditions)
        assert result == 1, f"ex_base returned {result} but expected 1."
        return result

query:
I'm trying to generate a list from my film data. My goal is to show each film's title next to the title of the film who comes exactly 4 positions after it in a simple, sequential ordering of all films. For instance, if the sequence is Film A, Film B, Film C, Film D, Film E, Film F..., I'd want to see pairs like (Film A, Film E), (Film B, Film F), etc.

I've been trying different ways to achieve this, involving ranking the films using various attributes and even grouping them first, but it feels like I'm making it much more complicated than it needs to be. My attempts seem to involve multiple steps and joins that aren't really related to just finding the film 4 positions down the line in a basic list. I'm getting tangled up in unnecessary details and not getting the straightforward pairing I'm looking for.

Could someone guide me on how to structure a query that simply pairs each film with the one 4 positions after it based on their fundamental order in the dataset? I just need the titles of the two films in each pair.
issue_sql: WITH FILMLIST AS (SELECT film_id, title, description, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY rating ORDER BY film_id) AS RATING_RANK, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY release_year, film_id) AS GLOBAL_RANK FROM film WHERE NOT length IS NULL) SELECT F1.title AS CURRENTFILM, F2.title AS FILMAFTER4 FROM FILMLIST AS F1 LEFT JOIN FILMLIST AS F2 ON F2.GLOBAL_RANK = F1.GLOBAL_RANK + 4 AND F2.rating = F1.rating WHERE F1.RATING_RANK <= 10 ORDER BY F1.GLOBAL_RANK
sol_sql: WITH FILMLIST AS (SELECT film_id, title, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY film_id) AS ROWNUMBER FROM film) SELECT F1.title AS CURRENTFILM, F2.title AS FILMAFTER4 FROM FILMLIST AS F1 LEFT JOIN FILMLIST AS F2 ON F2.ROWNUMBER = F1.ROWNUMBER + 4

The query runs without errors, but the output is completely wrong. I expected to see full names as strings, but instead I'm getting these numeric values. What's going on with the string concatenation here? Is there something specific about how SQLite handles this that I'm missing?
issue_sql: SELECT e1.first_name + ' ' + e1.last_name as employee_name, e2.first_name + ' ' + e2.last_name as manager_name FROM employee e1 LEFT JOIN employee e2 ON e1.reports_to = e2.employee_id

query: I'm trying to generate a report that shows daily order counts for a date range in our books database, even for days where there were no orders. My approach was to build a recursive CTE that generates 1-day intervals between a start and end date, then left join the cust_order table to count orders in each interval. The query runs but I'm only getting back 4 rows when I should be getting 5 days worth of data (Jan 1-5, 2020). It seems like the recursion is stopping one iteration too early and I'm missing the last day in my range. The intervals CTE appears to be generating from_time and to_time pairs, but the final interval from Jan 4 to Jan 5 never gets created. I've tried adjusting the WHERE clause in the recursive part but can't figure out why it's not including that last day interval. Is there something wrong with how I'm setting up the termination condition for the recursion?
issue_sql: WITH cte(min, max) AS (SELECT '2020-01-01 00:00:00', '2020-01-05 00:00:00'), intervals AS (SELECT min from_time, datetime(min, '+1 day') to_time, max FROM cte WHERE datetime(min, '+1 day') < max UNION ALL SELECT to_time, datetime(to_time, '+1 day'), max FROM intervals WHERE datetime(to_time, '+1 day') < max) SELECT i.from_time, i.to_time, COUNT(o.order_id) count FROM intervals i LEFT JOIN cust_order o ON o.order_date >= i.from_time AND o.order_date < i.to_time GROUP BY i.from_time, i.to_time;
sol_sql: WITH cte(min, max) AS (SELECT '2020-01-01 00:00:00', '2020-01-05 00:00:00'), intervals AS (SELECT min from_time, datetime(min, '+1 day') to_time, max FROM cte WHERE datetime(min, '+1 day') <= max UNION ALL SELECT to_time, datetime(to_time, '+1 day'), max FROM intervals WHERE datetime(to_time, '+1 day') <= max) SELECT i.from_time, i.to_time, COUNT(o.order_id) count FROM intervals i LEFT JOIN cust_order o ON o.order_date >= i.from_time AND o.order_date < i.to_time GROUP BY i.from_time, i.to_time;

I'm working with tv show and movie data, and I'm trying to figure out how many unique groupings of items exist based on their associated view summaries.

Essentially, I want to look at each view summary record, see which tv show(s) or movie(s) are linked to it, and then count how many distinct *sets* of tv shows/movies I find across all view summary records.

If view summary 3 is associated with just movie C, that's a different combination {C}. I need the final total count of these unique combinations.

I'm working with a database containing information about movies and tv shows and how they are paired up into 'view summaries'. I need help figuring out how to get a specific list of movie and tv show identifiers.

My goal is to find all movie and tv show identifiers that meet a couple of conditions relative to one particular movie, let's say the movie with identifier `1`. First, the list should definitely not include the movie identifier `1` itself. Second, it should also exclude any movie or tv show identifier that appears as the *second* movie or tv show in any view summary where movie identifier `1` is listed as the *first* movie.

There's one more condition: I only want to include movie and tv show identifiers in my final list if the corresponding view summary information (specifically, where the movie or tv show appears as the second one in a pair) indicates a missing or null view rank.

I've tried combining the movie, tv show, and view summary information and applying filters to exclude the pairs involving movie `1` as the first movie, but I'm not getting the right results. My list sometimes still contains movie `1`, or it includes movies or tv shows that are paired as the second movie or tv show with `1`, or it includes movies or tv shows associated with a view rank value when I only want those with a null/missing view rank. I'm finding it tricky to correctly exclude both the specific movie `1` and its specific pairings while also checking for that null view rank condition correctly.

How can I structure my query to get the list of movie and tv show identifiers that are *not* identifier `1`, are *not* the second movie or tv show in a view summary where `1` is the first movie, and only appear if the relevant view rank is null?

query:
I'm working with a dataset that tracks TV show information over time, including details like the show title and its availability globally, along with a timestamp for when the record was last modified. Each show is identified by a unique ID.

My goal is to generate a report that highlights changes in either the show title or the global availability for each show ID. I want to compare each record with the immediately preceding record for the same show ID (based on the modification timestamp) and list any differences found.

The output should clearly show the show ID, identify which field changed (either 'title' or 'available_globally'), the value before the change (the old value), and the value after the change (the new value).

I've been trying different approaches to order the records chronologically for each show and then compare adjacent records, but I'm running into trouble getting the correct pairings. Sometimes I seem to miss changes, or I compare records that aren't actually consecutive in time. I'm also finding it difficult to consistently compare the values, especially when dealing with potentially missing or empty information for the show title or global availability in older records.

How can I structure a query in SQLite to accurately compare each record with its immediate predecessor for the same show ID and report the specific changes in show title and global availability, including the old and new values?
issue_sql: WITH cte AS (SELECT id, title, available_globally, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY modified_date) AS rn FROM tv_show_history) SELECT c.id, 'title' AS columnname, p.title AS oldvalue, c.title AS newvalue FROM cte AS c LEFT JOIN cte AS p ON c.id = p.id WHERE COALESCE(c.title, '') <> COALESCE(p.title, '') UNION ALL SELECT c.id, 'available_globally' AS columnname, CAST(p.available_globally AS TEXT) AS oldvalue, CAST(c.available_globally AS TEXT) AS newvalue FROM cte AS c LEFT JOIN cte AS p ON c.id = p.id WHERE COALESCE(CAST(c.available_globally AS TEXT), '') <> COALESCE(CAST(p.available_globally AS TEXT), '') ORDER BY 1, 2
sol_sql: WITH cte AS (SELECT id, title, available_globally, modified_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY modified_date) AS rn FROM tv_show_history) SELECT c.id, 'title' AS columnname, p.title AS oldvalue, c.title AS newvalue FROM cte AS c JOIN cte AS p ON c.id = p.id AND p.rn + 1 = c.rn WHERE COALESCE(c.title, '') <> COALESCE(p.title, '') UNION ALL SELECT c.id, 'available_globally' AS columnname, CAST(p.available_globally AS TEXT) AS oldvalue, CAST(c.available_globally AS TEXT) AS newvalue FROM cte AS c JOIN cte AS p ON c.id = p.id AND p.rn + 1 = c.rn WHERE COALESCE(CAST(c.available_globally AS TEXT), '') <> COALESCE(CAST(p.available_globally AS TEXT), '') ORDER BY 1, 2

query: I'm working on an employee management report for our Chinook music store database and I need to list all employees along with their manager's name. I wrote a query that joins the employee table to itself to match each employee with their manager based on the reports_to field. The query works fine and returns the employee-manager pairs, but I'm noticing that our General Manager (Andrew Adams, who doesn't report to anyone) is completely missing from the results. I'm getting 7 rows back when I know we have 8 employees total in the company. The General Manager has a NULL value in his reports_to column since he's at the top of the hierarchy. I need him to show up in the report too, ideally with his manager_name showing as NULL or empty since he has no manager. How can I modify my approach to include employees who don't have a manager assigned?
issue_sql: SELECT e1.first_name || ' ' || e1.last_name as employee_name, e2.first_name || ' ' || e2.last_name as manager_name FROM employee e1 INNER JOIN employee e2 ON e1.reports_to = e2.employee_id

query:
I'm working with a database that stores information about films and the actors who star in them. My goal is to generate a report that lists each pair of actors who starred together in the same film. For each pair, I need to display the film identifier (like `film_id`), the identifiers and first names for both actors involved, and some property of the film they shared (like the film's `film_id` again as a connection identifier and its `rating`).

I have information about individual actors (like their names) and which films they acted in via a linking table (`film_actor`).

I've been trying to combine this information using joins, but I'm finding it tricky to get the structure right to identify pairs of actors within the same film. My attempts seem overly complicated. I want to ensure I retrieve a clear list showing each unique pair of co-stars with their details and the film's details.

How can I structure a query to correctly join the actor details and the film-actor relationship table to get the desired output showing film ID, actor 1 ID, actor 1 first name, actor 2 ID, actor 2 first name, the film ID (as connection ID), and the film's rating for each co-star pair?
issue_sql: WITH FilmDetails AS (SELECT film_id, title, rating FROM film WHERE length > 60), ActorPairBase AS (SELECT fa1.film_id, fa1.actor_id AS actor1_id, fa2.actor_id AS actor2_id FROM film_actor AS fa1 INNER JOIN film_actor AS fa2 ON fa1.film_id = fa2.film_id WHERE fa1.actor_id <> fa2.actor_id) SELECT apb.film_id, a1.actor_id AS actor1_id, a1.first_name AS actor1_name, a2.actor_id AS actor2_id, a2.first_name AS actor2_name, fd.film_id AS connection_film_id, fd.rating AS film_rating FROM ActorPairBase AS apb INNER JOIN actor AS a1 ON apb.actor1_id = a1.actor_id INNER JOIN actor AS a2 ON apb.actor2_id = a2.actor_id LEFT JOIN FilmDetails AS fd ON apb.film_id = fd.film_id WHERE NOT fd.film_id IS NULL ORDER BY apb.film_id, a1.actor_id, a2.actor_id
sol_sql: SELECT f.film_id, act1.actor_id AS actor1_id, act1.first_name AS actor1_name, act2.actor_id AS actor2_id, act2.first_name AS actor2_name, f.film_id AS connection_film_id, f.rating AS film_rating FROM film_actor AS fa1 INNER JOIN film_actor AS fa2 ON fa1.film_id = fa2.film_id AND fa1.actor_id < fa2.actor_id INNER JOIN actor AS act1 ON fa1.actor_id = act1.actor_id INNER JOIN actor AS act2 ON fa2.actor_id = act2.actor_id INNER JOIN film AS f ON fa1.film_id = f.film_id ORDER BY f.film_id, act1.actor_id, act2.actor_id

query: In the books database, we have a table named order_line that records book prices within specific orders. Each book can appear in multiple orders. For a specific range of orders identified by `order_id` (e.g., between 123 and 567), we need to calculate the trend in price for each book by subtracting the price associated with the end order (`order_id` 567) from the price associated with the start order (`order_id` 123). query:
I'm trying to query a music database to find albums that have multiple tracks with the same genre. Basically, I want to identify albums where the genre repeats across tracks, so I'm calculating the difference between total tracks and unique genres per album.

Here's my query:
issue_sql:
SELECT 
    album_id,
    COUNT(genre_id) AS total_tracks,
    COUNT(DISTINCT genre_id) AS unique_genres,
    (COUNT(genre_id) - COUNT(DISTINCT genre_id)) AS repeated_genres
FROM track
WHERE album_id IS NOT NULL AND genre_id IS NOT NULL AND repeated_genres > 0
GROUP BY album_id
ORDER BY repeated_genres DESC;
sol_sql:
SELECT 
    album_id,
    COUNT(genre_id) AS total_tracks,
    COUNT(DISTINCT genre_id) AS unique_genres,
    (COUNT(genre_id) - COUNT(DISTINCT genre_id)) AS repeated_genres
FROM track
WHERE album_id IS NOT NULL AND genre_id IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY album_id
HAVING repeated_genres > 0
ORDER BY repeated_genres DESC;

query: I'm working on a customer analytics query for our bookstore database and running into something weird. I need to calculate what percentage of each customer's orders have been cancelled or returned (status_id 5 or 6 in the order_history table). My query joins cust_order with order_history and uses a CASE statement inside AVG to calculate the cancellation rate per customer. The problem is that customers who have ONLY cancelled/returned orders are showing up fine, but customers with a mix of statuses are getting NULL for their cancellation_rate in the HAVING clause, so they're being filtered out even though they should be included. For example, customer 387 has 3 orders where 1 was cancelled, so they should show a 0.33 rate, but they're not appearing in my results at all. When I remove the HAVING clause I can see some customers have NULL rates when they definitely shouldn't. Is there something about how CASE works inside aggregate functions that I'm missing here?
issue_sql: SELECT co.customer_id, AVG(CASE WHEN oh.status_id = 5 OR oh.status_id = 6 THEN 1 END) AS cancellation_rate FROM cust_order co INNER JOIN order_history oh ON oh.order_id = co.order_id GROUP BY co.customer_id HAVING cancellation_rate > 0
sol_sql: SELECT co.customer_id, AVG(oh.status_id IN (5, 6)) AS cancellation_rate FROM cust_order co INNER JOIN order_history oh ON oh.order_id = co.order_id GROUP BY co.customer_id HAVING cancellation_rate > 0;
test_cases:
def test_case(pred_sqls, sol_sqls, db_path, conn, conditions):
    result = ex_base(pred_sqls, sol_sqls, db_path, conn, conditions)
    assert result == 1, "Query results do not match"
    
    # Additional assertion to verify the cancellation_rate values are correct
    # The buggy SQL returns 1.0 for all rows because CASE without ELSE returns NULL for non-matching cases
    # AVG(NULL) in those positions causes incorrect calculation
    # The correct SQL uses IN operator which returns 0 or 1, giving proper average
    pred_result, p_err, p_to = execute_queries(pred_sqls, db_path, conn, logger=None)
    assert not p_err and not p_to, "Prediction query failed"
    assert pred_result is not None and len(pred_result) > 0, "No prediction results"
    
    # Check that at least one cancellation_rate is less than 1.0
    # The buggy SQL incorrectly returns 1.0 for all rows
    cancellation_rates = [row[1] for row in pred_result]
    assert any(rate < 1.0 for rate in cancellation_rates), "All cancellation rates are 1.0, indicating buggy CASE statement without ELSE clause"
    
    # Verify that cancellation rates are between 0 and 1
    assert all(0 < rate <= 1.0 for rate in cancellation_rates), f"Cancellation rates out of valid range: {cancellation_rates[:5]}"
    
    return 1

I've tried a few ways to structure this query, particularly focusing on how to aggregate the payment identifiers into an array for each customer within the JSON structure. However, I haven't been successful in getting the desired format. My attempts either resulted in errors or didn't produce the nested array structure correctly alongside the customer identifier.

How can I construct a query in SQLite to produce a JSON object for each active customer (who has payments), where each object contains the customer ID and a corresponding JSON array of their payment IDs?
issue_sql: SELECT json_object('customer_id', c.customer_id, 'payments', json_group_array(t.payment_id)) FROM customer c INNER JOIN payment t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id WHERE c.active = 1

I'm working with movie rental data and trying to identify specific films based on their rating and associated actors. My goal is to get a list of film identifiers for films which have a rating of 'PG'. Additionally, these films must have associated actor records indicating a specific actor, let's say actor ID 1.

The tricky part is that I only want to include films if this *exact combination* – having the 'PG' rating *and* actor ID 1 in an actor record – occurs more than once across all the records associated with films having that 'PG' rating.

I've tried looking at films with the 'PG' rating and counting occurrences, but that doesn't seem to correctly factor in the actor ID from the film-actor data. I'm struggling to figure out how to check for multiple instances of this specific *pair* of conditions ('PG' rating and actor ID 1) together.

How can I structure a query to retrieve the identifiers for films which have a 'PG' rating and have actor ID 1 in their film-actor details, but only when this specific combination ('PG' rating and actor ID 1) appears multiple times in the dataset?
issue_sql: SELECT COUNT(rating), rating FROM film WHERE rating = 'PG' GROUP BY rating HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
sol_sql: SELECT f.film_id, f.rating FROM film AS f JOIN film_actor AS fa ON (f.film_id = fa.film_id) JOIN (SELECT f.rating, fa.actor_id FROM film AS f JOIN film_actor AS fa ON (f.film_id = fa.film_id) WHERE f.rating = 'PG' AND fa.actor_id = 1 GROUP BY f.rating, fa.actor_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1) AS j ON (f.rating = j.rating AND fa.actor_id = j.actor_id)

query: I'm working with a books database and trying to find all books that have the same page count as at least two other books. My approach was to count how many books share each page count value, then filter to only show books where that count is greater than 2. The query runs without errors, but I'm getting way more rows back than I expected - it's returning thousands of results when I know there should only be a few hundred books that actually meet this criteria. When I look at the output, I see books with unique page counts (like a book with 457 pages where no other book has that count) still showing up in my results with similar_page_count showing as 1, even though my WHERE clause should be filtering those out. I've tried wrapping it in a subquery thinking that would let me filter on the window function result, but the filtering just isn't working. What am I missing here about how to properly filter results based on a window function calculation?
issue_sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT book_id, title, num_pages, COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY num_pages) as similar_page_count FROM book) WHERE similar_page_count > 2
sol_sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT book_id, title, num_pages, COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY num_pages RANGE BETWEEN 10 PRECEDING AND 10 FOLLOWING) as similar_page_count FROM book) WHERE similar_page_count > 2

    # Additional assertion to verify the specific difference in similar_page_count
    # The gt_sql uses RANGE BETWEEN which counts books within +/- 10 pages
    # The issue_sql uses PARTITION BY which only counts exact page matches
    pred_result, p_err, p_to = execute_queries(pred_sqls, db_path, conn, logger=None)
    assert not p_err and not p_to, "Prediction query failed"

    # For books with num_pages=0, gt_sql should have higher count (212) than issue_sql (77)
    pred_first_count = pred_result[0][3]
    sol_first_count = sol_result[0][3]
    
    assert pred_first_count == sol_first_count, f"similar_page_count mismatch: predicted {pred_first_count}, expected {sol_first_count}"
    
    return 1

query: In the database chinook, create a table that lists each player, the date they joined a team, and the date they left the team based on the player_movements table.
issue_sql: SELECT player_name, event_date AS join_date, (SELECT MIN(event_date) FROM player_movements AS pm1 WHERE pm1.player_name = pm.player_name AND pm1.event = 'Leave') AS leave_date FROM player_movements AS pm WHERE event = 'Join'
sol_sql: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS player_team_history;

CREATE TABLE player_team_history AS SELECT player_name, event_date AS joined_date, (SELECT MIN(event_date) FROM player_movements AS pm1 WHERE pm1.player_name = pm.player_name AND pm1.event = 'Leave' AND pm1.event_date > pm.event_date) AS left_date FROM player_movements AS pm WHERE event = 'Join';

query:
I'm trying to write a SQLite query that retrieves film information along with the count of actors and the most recently added actor's full name for each film. My query runs without throwing an error, but the `most_recent_actor` column is returning `0` for all rows instead of the actor's name.

Here's my query:
issue_sql:
SELECT 
    f.film_id,
    f.title,
    f.description,
    f.release_year,
    f.rental_rate,
    f.length,
    f.rating,
    (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT actor_id) 
     FROM film_actor 
     WHERE film_id = f.film_id) as actor_count,
    (SELECT first_name + ' ' + last_name
     FROM actor a
     INNER JOIN film_actor fa ON a.actor_id = fa.actor_id
     WHERE fa.film_id = f.film_id
     ORDER BY fa.last_update DESC
     LIMIT 1) as most_recent_actor,
    (SELECT c.name
     FROM category c
     INNER JOIN film_category fc ON c.category_id = fc.category_id
     WHERE fc.film_id = f.film_id
     ORDER BY fc.last_update DESC
     LIMIT 1) as most_recent_category
FROM film f
INNER JOIN film_actor fa ON fa.film_id = f.film_id
GROUP BY f.film_id, f.title, f.description, f.release_year, f.rental_rate, f.length, f.rating;

     FROM actor a
     INNER JOIN film_actor fa ON a.actor_id = fa.actor_id

INNER JOIN film_actor fa ON fa.film_id = f.film_id

In that case, I need to look at the film after that, and so on, until I find the first subsequent film that actually has a description recorded. I've tried a couple of ways to look ahead to the next record to grab the description, but my attempts haven't quite handled the situation where multiple consecutive films might be missing descriptions. It seems like I'm only getting the value from the very next row, regardless of whether it's missing or not, instead of finding the *first available* description from the following records. How can I structure a query to correctly fill in the missing descriptions for a film using the description from the first subsequent film that has a non-missing description, based on the film identifier order?

query:
I'm working with customer and invoice data in SQLite and trying to summarize information for each customer. My goal is to get a count of all invoices associated with each customer, and also separate counts for invoices where a specific condition (let's call it 'total') was recorded as either present (value 1) or absent (value 0).

I have records linking customers to their various invoices over time. The issue I'm running into is handling cases where the total status for a particular invoice record might be missing or null. When I try to aggregate this information, my attempts seem to incorrectly filter out customers or miscount the total invoices if any of the total statuses are null. I want to make sure I get the total count of *all* invoices for every customer, regardless of the total status, alongside the specific counts for when total was explicitly recorded as 1 or 0.

For example, if a customer had 3 invoices, one with total=1, one with total=0, and one where the total status is unknown (null), I'd expect to see that customer listed with a total invoice count of 3, a total count of 1, and a no-total count of 1. My current approaches don't seem to achieve this correctly; they either drop the customer or don't give the right total count.

How can I structure my query to correctly count the total number of invoices per customer, while also separately counting invoices explicitly marked with total status 1 and status 0 using conditional aggregation, ensuring customers aren't excluded just because some of their invoice records have a null status for total?
issue_sql: WITH CustomerInvoiceData AS (SELECT C.customer_id AS CUSTOMERID, C.first_name, C.last_name, I.invoice_date, I.billing_country, I.total, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY C.customer_id ORDER BY I.invoice_date) AS InvoiceRank FROM customer AS C LEFT JOIN invoice AS I ON C.customer_id = I.customer_id), LatestTrackResults AS (SELECT T.track_id, T.unit_price, T.genre_id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY T.track_id ORDER BY T.name DESC) AS TrackRank FROM track AS T WHERE T.unit_price <= 10), CustomerRiskProfile AS (SELECT CID.CUSTOMERID, CID.first_name, CID.last_name, CID.invoice_date, CID.billing_country, CID.total, CASE WHEN LTR.unit_price < 1 OR LTR.genre_id > 10 THEN 'High' WHEN CID.total IS NULL THEN 'Unknown' ELSE 'Normal' END AS RiskCategory FROM CustomerInvoiceData AS CID LEFT JOIN LatestTrackResults AS LTR ON CID.CUSTOMERID = LTR.track_id AND LTR.TrackRank = 1) SELECT CUSTOMERID, first_name, last_name, COUNT(invoice_date) AS INVOICECOUNT, SUM(CASE WHEN total = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS TOTALCOUNT, SUM(CASE WHEN total = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS NOTOTALCOUNT, MAX(CASE WHEN RiskCategory = 'High' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS HighRiskFlag FROM CustomerRiskProfile WHERE RiskCategory <> 'Unknown' GROUP BY CUSTOMERID, first_name, last_name HAVING COUNT(invoice_date) > 0 ORDER BY SUM(CASE WHEN total = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) DESC, CUSTOMERID
sol_sql: SELECT C.customer_id AS CUSTOMERID, C.first_name, C.last_name, COUNT(I.invoice_id) AS INVOICECOUNT, SUM(CASE WHEN I.total = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS TOTALCOUNT, SUM(CASE WHEN I.total = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS NOTOTALCOUNT FROM customer AS C LEFT JOIN invoice AS I ON C.customer_id = I.customer_id GROUP BY C.customer_id, C.first_name, C.last_name ORDER BY C.customer_id

query: I'm building a recommendation feature for our Netflix clone where I need to show "similar shows" by pairing up all TV shows with each other. My query joins the tv_show table to itself to get all possible combinations, but I'm getting way more rows than expected. For example, with just 10 shows in the database, I'm getting 90 pairs back instead of the 45 unique pairs I actually need. The problem is I'm seeing both "Show A paired with Show B" and "Show B paired with Show A" as separate rows, which creates duplicates from a business logic perspective. I only need each unique pair once, not both directions. How can I modify my self-join condition to eliminate these mirror duplicate pairs while still getting all the unique show combinations?
issue_sql: SELECT t1.title, t2.title FROM tv_show t1 JOIN tv_show t2 ON t1.id != t2.id
sol_sql: SELECT t1.title, t2.title FROM tv_show t1 JOIN tv_show t2 ON t1.id < t2.id
test_cases:
def test_case(pred_sqls, sol_sqls, db_path, conn, conditions):
    # Get the result from pred_sqls (already executed by framework)
    pred_result = pred_query_result
    
    # Execute sol_sqls to get the expected result
    sol_result, s_err, s_to = execute_queries(sol_sqls, db_path, conn, logger=None)
    
    assert not s_err and not s_to, "Solution query execution failed"
    assert sol_result is not None, "Solution query returned None"
    
    # The key difference: gt_sql uses t1.id < t2.id which produces N*(N-1)/2 unique pairs
    # issue_sql uses t1.id != t2.id which produces N*(N-1) pairs (includes both (A,B) and (B,A))
    
    # Check that pred_result has the correct number of rows
    assert pred_result is not None, "Prediction query returned None"
    
    pred_count = len(pred_result)
    sol_count = len(sol_result)
    
    # issue_sql should produce exactly 2x the rows of gt_sql (duplicate pairs in reverse order)
    assert pred_count == sol_count, f"Row count mismatch: got {pred_count}, expected {sol_count}. issue_sql produces duplicate pairs with != operator."
    
    # Convert to sets to check for duplicates when order is reversed
    pred_set = set(pred_result)
    sol_set = set(sol_result)
    
    # Check that all solution pairs are in prediction
    assert sol_set.issubset(pred_set), "Prediction missing expected pairs"
    
    # For gt_sql, no pair should have its reverse also present
    # For issue_sql, every pair (a,b) should have (b,a) also present
    reverse_pairs_in_pred = set()
    for title1, title2 in pred_result:
        if (title2, title1) in pred_set:
            reverse_pairs_in_pred.add((title1, title2))
    
    # gt_sql should have no reverse pairs (or minimal due to id < constraint)
    # issue_sql should have many reverse pairs
    assert len(reverse_pairs_in_pred) == 0, f"Found {len(reverse_pairs_in_pred)} pairs with their reverse also present - indicates != operator was used instead of <"
    
    return 1

query: In the books database, we have a timeseries of order status changes recorded potentially at 5-minute intervals (based on sample data). Each status change is associated with a specific order and a status ID. The goal is to produce a list of valid time intervals for each order and status ID combination.

sol_sql: WITH ranked_records AS (SELECT status_date, order_id, status_id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY order_id, status_id ORDER BY status_date) AS rn, LAG(status_date) OVER (PARTITION BY order_id, status_id ORDER BY status_date) AS prev_date FROM order_history_temp), gap_detection AS (SELECT status_date, order_id, status_id, rn, prev_date, CASE WHEN prev_date IS NULL THEN 1 WHEN CAST((julianday(status_date) - julianday(prev_date)) * 24 * 60 AS INTEGER) > 5 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_gap FROM ranked_records), period_groups AS (SELECT status_date, order_id, status_id, SUM(is_gap) OVER (PARTITION BY order_id, status_id ORDER BY rn) AS period_id FROM gap_detection) SELECT order_id, status_id, MIN(status_date) AS period_start, MAX(status_date) AS period_end FROM period_groups GROUP BY order_id, status_id, period_id ORDER BY order_id, status_id, period_start

query:
I'm trying to rank customers by their total spending in a SQLite database and then filter for a specific customer to see their rank. My query uses nested subqueries to calculate the total spent per customer, apply a ranking, and then filter by customer_id.

Here's my SQL:
issue_sql:
SELECT * 
FROM (
  SELECT 
    customer_id,
    first_name,
    last_name,
    total_spent,
    RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_spent DESC) AS spending_rank
  FROM (
    SELECT 
      c.customer_id,
      c.first_name,
      c.last_name,
      COALESCE(SUM(i.total), 0) AS total_spent
    FROM customer c
    LEFT JOIN invoice i ON c.customer_id = i.customer_id
    WHERE SUM(i.total) > 0
    GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name
  )
) ranked_customers
WHERE customer_id = 5;
sol_sql:
SELECT * 
FROM (
  SELECT 
    customer_id,
    first_name,
    last_name,
    total_spent,
    RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_spent DESC) AS spending_rank
  FROM (
    SELECT 
      c.customer_id,
      c.first_name,
      c.last_name,
      COALESCE(SUM(i.total), 0) AS total_spent
    FROM customer c
    LEFT JOIN invoice i ON c.customer_id = i.customer_id
    GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name
  )
) ranked_customers
WHERE customer_id = 5;

query:
I'm working with some movie and tv show data in SQLite and trying to understand the distribution of view data across different content types. I have one set of data listing movie and tv show identifiers and their types (movie or tv show), and another set containing view details linked by the same identifiers.

My goal is to generate a summary for each content type. For every type, I need to see:
1. How many content identifiers of that type have corresponding entries in the view details data.
2. The total number of content identifiers listed for that type overall.
3. The percentage calculated from the first count divided by the second count.

I've managed to figure out how to get the first part – counting the identifiers for each type that are present in the view details data. However, I'm stuck trying to also include the total count for each type and calculate the percentage within the same query result. I'm not sure how to combine these counts correctly to get the final summary I need.

How can I structure my query to get all three pieces of information (the count of matching identifiers, the total count per type, and the resulting percentage) together for each content type?
issue_sql: SELECT 'TV Show' as content_type, COUNT(*) AS matching_count FROM tv_show WHERE id IN (SELECT tv_show_id FROM season) GROUP BY content_type
sol_sql: WITH tv_show_stats AS (SELECT 'TV Show' as content_type, COUNT(DISTINCT a.id) AS total_count, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.id IS NOT NULL THEN a.id END) AS matching_count FROM tv_show AS a LEFT JOIN season AS b ON a.id = b.tv_show_id LEFT JOIN view_summary AS c ON b.id = c.season_id), movie_stats AS (SELECT 'Movie' as content_type, COUNT(DISTINCT m.id) AS total_count, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN v.id IS NOT NULL THEN m.id END) AS matching_count FROM movie m LEFT JOIN view_summary v ON m.id = v.movie_id) SELECT content_type, matching_count, total_count, CASE WHEN total_count > 0 THEN ROUND((matching_count * 100.0) / total_count, 2) ELSE 0 END AS percentage FROM (SELECT * FROM tv_show_stats UNION ALL SELECT * FROM movie_stats) ORDER BY matching_count DESC

query:
I'm working with some data where I need to pull specific credit values based on choices made. I have one set of data that links an ID to various credit amounts across different categories – think Art, Business, Nature, etc., all in separate columns for each ID. Then, I have another set of data recording which category each ID actually chose at different times.

My goal is to generate a list showing each ID, the category they chose, and the *specific* credit value associated with *that chosen category* for that ID. For example, if ID 5001 chose 'ART', I need the result row to show ID 5001, 'ART', and whatever the credit value is in the Art credit column for ID 5001. If the same ID also made a choice for 'BIZ', I'd expect another row showing that choice and the corresponding Business credit value.

I've tried joining these pieces of information and using conditional logic to figure out which credit column to pull from based on the choice made. However, I'm running into issues where the results don't seem right – I might be getting summed values or results that don't accurately reflect the single credit value for the specific choice. It feels like maybe I'm aggregating incorrectly or making the join more complex than it needs to be.

How can I structure my query to correctly link each ID's choice to the corresponding credit value from the correct category column, ensuring I get just that specific value for each choice record?
issue_sql: WITH latest_credits AS (SELECT C.ID, C.ARTCRED, C.BIZCRED, C.HUMCRED, C.NATCRED, C.TEKCRED, C.GENCRED, C.EFFECTIVE_DATE, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY C.ID ORDER BY C.EFFECTIVE_DATE DESC) AS RN FROM credits AS C) SELECT G.ID, G.GROUPCHOICE, SUM(CASE WHEN G.GROUPCHOICE = 'ART' AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE THEN LC.ARTCRED WHEN G.GROUPCHOICE = 'BIZ' AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE THEN LC.BIZCRED WHEN G.GROUPCHOICE = 'HUM' AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE THEN LC.HUMCRED WHEN G.GROUPCHOICE = 'NAT' AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE THEN LC.NATCRED WHEN G.GROUPCHOICE = 'TEK' AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE THEN LC.TEKCRED WHEN G.GROUPCHOICE = 'GEN' AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE THEN LC.GENCRED ELSE 0 END) AS TOTAL_CREDITS FROM grp_choices AS G JOIN latest_credits AS LC ON G.ID = LC.ID GROUP BY G.ID, G.GROUPCHOICE ORDER BY G.ID, G.GROUPCHOICE

ID, 15 AS ARTCRED, 9 AS BIZCRED, 14 AS HUMCRED, 16 AS NATCRED, 13 AS TEKCRED, 11 AS GENCRED, DATE('2020-01-01') AS EFFECTIVE_DATE UNION ALL SELECT 5002 AS ID, 10.5 AS ARTCRED, 6 AS BIZCRED, 5 AS HUMCRED, 4 AS NATCRED, 6 AS TEKCRED, 3 AS GENCRED, DATE('2015-01-01') AS EFFECTIVE_DATE), "grp_choices" AS (SELECT 5001 AS ID, 'ART' AS GROUPCHOICE, DATE('2010-03-03') AS DATECHOICE UNION ALL SELECT 5001 AS ID, 'BIZ' AS GROUPCHOICE, DATE('2015-05-05') AS DATECHOICE UNION ALL SELECT 5001 AS ID, 'NAT' AS GROUPCHOICE, DATE('2022-06-23') AS DATECHOICE UNION ALL SELECT 5002 AS ID, 'ART' AS GROUPCHOICE, DATE('2023-06-23') AS DATECHOICE), "latest_credits" AS (SELECT C.ID, C.ARTCRED, C.BIZCRED, C.HUMCRED, C.NATCRED, C.TEKCRED, C.GENCRED, C.EFFECTIVE_DATE, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY C.ID ORDER BY C.EFFECTIVE_DATE DESC) AS RN FROM "credits" AS C WHERE C.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= (SELECT MAX(G.DATECHOICE) FROM "grp_choices" AS G WHERE G.ID = C.ID)) SELECT G.ID, G.GROUPCHOICE, CASE G.GROUPCHOICE WHEN 'ART' THEN (SELECT LC.ARTCRED FROM "latest_credits" AS LC WHERE LC.ID = G.ID AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE ORDER BY LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE DESC LIMIT 1) WHEN 'BIZ' THEN (SELECT LC.BIZCRED FROM "latest_credits" AS LC WHERE LC.ID = G.ID AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE ORDER BY LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE DESC LIMIT 1) WHEN 'HUM' THEN (SELECT LC.HUMCRED FROM "latest_credits" AS LC WHERE LC.ID = G.ID AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE ORDER BY LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE DESC LIMIT 1) WHEN 'NAT' THEN (SELECT LC.NATCRED FROM "latest_credits" AS LC WHERE LC.ID = G.ID AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE ORDER BY LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE DESC LIMIT 1) WHEN 'TEK' THEN (SELECT LC.TEKCRED FROM "latest_credits" AS LC WHERE LC.ID = G.ID AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE ORDER BY LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE DESC LIMIT 1) WHEN 'GEN' THEN (SELECT LC.GENCRED FROM "latest_credits" AS LC WHERE LC.ID = G.ID AND LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE <= G.DATECHOICE ORDER BY LC.EFFECTIVE_DATE DESC LIMIT 1) ELSE 0 END AS CREDIT FROM "grp_choices" AS G ORDER BY G.ID, G.GROUPCHOICE

query: I'm pulling the 10 most recent movies from our Netflix database to display on a dashboard, but the results are completely out of order. query: In the context of analyzing invoice data, I'm attempting to calculate the average total amount for each customer, grouped by the hour of the invoice. The goal is to understand the purchase trends of customers at different times of the day without resorting to external scripting. Here's the initial approach I took, which unfortunately resulted in an error due to incorrect handling of the timestamp data.
issue_sql: SELECT customer_id, AVG(total) AS avg_total, SUM(total) AS total_amount, MAX(total) AS max_total, MIN(total) AS min_total, COUNT(customer_id) AS count FROM invoice GROUP BY customer_id, EXTRACT(hour FROM invoice_date)
sol_sql: SELECT strftime('%H', invoice_date) AS hour, customer_id, AVG(total) AS avg_total, SUM(total) AS total_amount, MAX(total) AS max_total, MIN(total) AS min_total, COUNT(customer_id) AS count FROM invoice GROUP BY strftime('%H', invoice_date), customer_id
test_cases:
def test_case(pred_sqls, sol_sqls, db_path, conn, conditions):
    # Create a temporary database connection since the error indicates no ephemeral database is available
    import sqlite3
    temp_conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
    
    # Copy schema and data from the main database to the temporary one
    with sqlite3.connect(db_path) as src_conn:
        for line in src_conn.iterdump():
            if line.strip() != '':
                try:
                    temp_conn.execute(line)
                except sqlite3.Error:
                    pass
    
    # Now use the temporary connection for testing
    pred_sqls = remove_distinct(pred_sqls)
    sol_sqls = remove_distinct(sol_sqls)
    
    # Execute the solution SQL to get expected results
    sol_results = []
    for sql in sol_sqls:
        try:
            cursor = temp_conn.execute(sql)
            sol_results.append((cursor.fetchall(), [desc[0] for desc in cursor.description]))
        except Exception as e:
            assert False, f"Solution SQL failed: {e}"
    
    # For issue_sql, we expect it to fail because EXTRACT is not supported in SQLite
    if pred_sqls != sol_sqls:
        try:
            for sql in pred_sqls:
                temp_conn.execute(sql)
            # If we get here, the issue_sql unexpectedly succeeded
            assert False, "Issue SQL should fail but it passed"
        except:
            # This is expected for issue_sql
            pass
        return 1
    
    # For sol_sql, we expect it to succeed
    pred_results = []
    for sql in pred_sqls:
        try:
            cursor = temp_conn.execute(sql)
            pred_results.append((cursor.fetchall(), [desc[0] for desc in cursor.description]))
        except Exception as e:
            assert False, f"Prediction SQL failed: {e}"
    
    # Compare results
    assert len(pred_results) == len(sol_results), "Different number of result sets"
    for i in range(len(pred_results)):
        pred_data, pred_cols = pred_results[i]
        sol_data, sol_cols = sol_results[i]
        
        assert pred_cols == sol_cols, f"Column names don't match: {pred_cols} vs {sol_cols}"
        
        # Convert to sets for unordered comparison
        pred_set = set(tuple(row) for row in pred_data)
        sol_set = set(tuple(row) for row in sol_data)
        assert pred_set == sol_set, "Result data doesn't match"
    
    temp_conn.close()
    return 1

query: In the context of analyzing customer payments, I have the `payment` table ordered by the `payment_id` column. I aim to perform an aggregation (sum of `amount`) on `n` rows at a time but also wish to incorporate the previous `k` and the next `k'` rows as context. For instance, considering the first 6 payments for `customer_id = 1`, ordered by `payment_id`, I want to aggregate `n` rows with overlapping context from adjacent rows. An example query to get the sum of `amount` for `n=2` would group the rows into pairs based on their order. However, achieving overlapping groups, say with `k = 2` preceding and `k' = 3` following rows, to get sums for specific overlapping windows proves challenging. Is there a way to accomplish this using window functions with frame specifications to define the range of rows before and after the current row? The output should contain only one column 'running_sum', which stores the aggregation values for specific rows based on the window function.
issue_sql: WITH payment_subset AS (SELECT payment_id, customer_id, amount, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY payment_id) AS rn FROM payment WHERE customer_id = 1 ORDER BY payment_id LIMIT 6) SELECT SUM(amount) FROM payment_subset GROUP BY (rn - 1) / 2
sol_sql: WITH payment_subset AS (SELECT payment_id, customer_id, amount, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY payment_id) AS rn FROM payment WHERE customer_id = 1 ORDER BY payment_id LIMIT 6), running AS (SELECT rn, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY rn ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND 3 FOLLOWING) AS running_sum FROM payment_subset) SELECT running_sum FROM running WHERE (rn - 1) % 2 = 0

query: In the movie_3 database, we will simulate an employee hierarchy using a temporary table `employee_hierarchy`. Each employee has an `employee_id`, a `manager_id`, and a `salary`. The task is to find, for each employee, the lowest-ranked indirect manager in the hierarchy who earns at least twice as much as the employee. An attempt to create a recursive CTE to establish the hierarchy is struggling to find the correct indirect manager based on the salary condition. The user's query is provided below and is producing incorrect results. In the final result, we need the employeeId, employeeSalary, hierarchical_level, bossId, bossSalary where the boss is the first one whose salary is at least twice as much as the employee.
issue_sql: WITH RECURSIVE EmpMgrCTE AS (SELECT employee_id, manager_id, salary, 0 AS EmployeeLevel FROM employee_hierarchy WHERE manager_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT emp.employee_id, emp.manager_id, emp.salary, mgr.EmployeeLevel + 1 AS EmployeeLevel FROM employee_hierarchy AS emp INNER JOIN EmpMgrCTE AS mgr ON emp.manager_id = mgr.employee_id) SELECT * FROM EmpMgrCTE AS emp
sol_sql: WITH RECURSIVE EmpMgrCTE AS (SELECT employee_id, manager_id, salary, 0 AS EmployeeLevel, employee_id AS baseId, salary AS baseSalary FROM employee_hierarchy WHERE NOT manager_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT emp.employee_id, emp.manager_id, emp.salary, mgr.EmployeeLevel + 1 AS EmployeeLevel, mgr.baseId, mgr.baseSalary FROM employee_hierarchy AS emp INNER JOIN EmpMgrCTE AS mgr ON emp.employee_id = mgr.manager_id WHERE baseSalary * 2 > mgr.salary) SELECT emp.baseId, emp.baseSalary, EmployeeLevel, emp.employee_id AS bossid, emp.salary AS bossSalary FROM EmpMgrCTE AS emp WHERE baseSalary * 2 <= salary ORDER BY baseId

query:
I'm trying to calculate the average number of customers and invoices per day of the week in SQLite using the chinook database. My approach is to first group invoices by date to get daily counts, then group those results by day of week to get averages.

Here's my query:
issue_sql:
SELECT 
  day_number,
  AVG(customer_count) as avg_customers_per_day,
  AVG(invoice_count) as avg_invoices_per_day
FROM (
  SELECT 
    STRFTIME('%w', invoice_date) as day_number,
    DATE(invoice_date) as invoice_day,
    COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) as customer_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT invoice_id) as invoice_count
  FROM invoice
  GROUP BY DATE(invoice_date)
)
GROUP BY STRFTIME('%w', invoice_date)
ORDER BY day_number;
sol_sql:
SELECT 
  day_number,
  AVG(customer_count) as avg_customers_per_day,
  AVG(invoice_count) as avg_invoices_per_day
FROM (
  SELECT 
    STRFTIME('%w', invoice_date) as day_number,
    DATE(invoice_date) as invoice_day,
    COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) as customer_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT invoice_id) as invoice_count
  FROM invoice
  GROUP BY DATE(invoice_date)
)
GROUP BY day_number
ORDER BY day_number;

query: I'm working with a movie rental database and trying to find the top 2 categories where actors have appeared in at least 2 films within that category. My query joins film_actor, film_category, and category tables, groups by category name and actor_id, filters for actors with 2+ films per category, and then I'm trying to count how many such actor-category combinations exist total. The problem is that my SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER () is giving me weird inflated numbers - like if there are 50 qualifying actor-category pairs, I'm getting something like 500 or more in the count column. Each row in my result set shows a different category name but the count column keeps showing this same huge inflated number across all rows. I need to get the actual total number of distinct categories that have at least one actor with 2+ films, but instead I'm getting what looks like some kind of multiplication effect happening. The LIMIT 2 at the end is supposed to give me just the top 2 categories by this count, but since all the counts are the same inflated value, it's not really sorting meaningfully. What's going wrong with my window function here?
issue_sql: SELECT DISTINCT c.name AS category, SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER () AS count FROM film_actor fa JOIN film_category fc ON fa.film_id = fc.film_id JOIN category c ON fc.category_id = c.category_id GROUP BY c.name, fa.actor_id HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2 ORDER BY count DESC LIMIT 2;
sol_sql: SELECT DISTINCT c.name AS category, SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (PARTITION BY c.name) AS count FROM film_actor fa JOIN film_category fc ON fa.film_id = fc.film_id JOIN category c ON fc.category_id = c.category_id GROUP BY c.name, fa.actor_id HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2 ORDER BY count DESC LIMIT 2;

sol_sql: WITH combined_dates AS (SELECT release_date FROM movie WHERE release_date IS NOT NULL UNION ALL SELECT release_date FROM tv_show WHERE release_date IS NOT NULL), ranked_dates AS (SELECT release_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY release_date) as rn, COUNT(*) OVER () as total FROM combined_dates) SELECT CASE WHEN total % 2 = 1 THEN (SELECT release_date FROM ranked_dates WHERE rn = (total + 1) / 2) ELSE (SELECT (julianday(r1.release_date) + julianday(r2.release_date)) / 2 FROM ranked_dates r1, ranked_dates r2 WHERE r1.rn = r1.total / 2 AND r2.rn = r1.total / 2 + 1) END as median_release_date FROM ranked_dates LIMIT 1

For a specific film, let's say the one with ID 1, I need to retrieve its title along with two consolidated strings: one containing all the unique identifiers of actors who starred in it, separated by semicolons, and another string containing all the associated film category names, also separated by semicolons. My initial attempt involved bringing together the film information, the actor records, and the category details for that film. I then tried to aggregate the actor identifiers and the category names into their respective semicolon-separated strings. However, the output isn't what I expected. The resulting strings have duplicate entries. For instance, it looks like each actor identifier is repeated for every category linked to the film, leading to a much longer string than it should be. I just want a distinct list of actors and a distinct list of film categories. I'm confused about why combining these related pieces of information is causing this duplication when I try to aggregate them into strings. How can I structure my query to correctly retrieve the film title, a single string with all unique actor identifiers, and a single string with all unique film category names for the film with ID 1, ensuring there are no duplicates in the generated lists? The solution involves aggregating the actor identifiers and category names separately using subqueries or CTEs before combining them, so that the many-to-many relationships between actors and categories don't cause the Cartesian product that's creating your duplicates.
issue_sql: SELECT T1.film_id, T1.title, GROUP_CONCAT(CAST(T2.actor_id AS TEXT), ';') AS actors, GROUP_CONCAT(T4.name, ';') AS film_categories FROM film AS T1 LEFT JOIN film_actor AS T2 ON T1.film_id = T2.film_id LEFT JOIN film_category AS T3 ON T1.film_id = T3.film_id LEFT JOIN category AS T4 ON T3.category_id = T4.category_id WHERE T1.film_id = 1 GROUP BY T1.film_id, T1.title
sol_sql: SELECT T1.film_id, T1.title, a.tmp_col AS "actors", b.tmp_col AS "film_categories" FROM film AS T1 LEFT JOIN (SELECT film_id, GROUP_CONCAT(CAST(actor_id AS TEXT), ';') AS tmp_col FROM film_actor GROUP BY film_id) AS a ON T1.film_id = a.film_id LEFT JOIN (SELECT T2.film_id, GROUP_CONCAT(T3.name, ';') AS tmp_col FROM film_category AS T2 JOIN category AS T3 ON T2.category_id = T3.category_id GROUP BY T2.film_id) AS b ON T1.film_id = b.film_id WHERE T1.film_id = 1

I'm working with a dataset containing film records, where each film has a unique ID and various numerical attributes. I need to analyze a specific subset of these attributes for each film record: rental duration, rental rate, length, replacement cost, and language ID.

My goal is to identify, for each film ID, which three of these five attributes have the highest values. I want to see the film ID, the three highest numerical values from that specific group of five attributes, and also the names of those three attributes (like 'length', 'replacement_cost', 'rental_duration').

I've tried grouping the data by the film ID, but I seem to only be able to pull out the single highest value from the five attributes for each record, not the top three. I'm also finding it tricky to correctly display the names of the attributes alongside their corresponding top values for each film.

How can I structure a query to get the top three values *and* their corresponding attribute names from that specific list (rental duration, rental rate, length, replacement cost, language ID) for each film ID? query:
I'm trying to find all films that were NOT rented or returned during a specific date range (May 24 to June 15, 2005) in our movie rental database. My query uses a NOT EXISTS subquery that joins rental and inventory tables to check for any rentals in that period, but something is really wrong with the results. 

The query is returning ALL films in the database, even though I know for certain that many films were definitely rented during this timeframe. When I run just the inner subquery by itself, it returns thousands of rental records as expected, so there's clearly rental activity. But when I wrap it in the NOT EXISTS clause, it's like the correlation isn't working at all - every single film_id comes back as if none of them had any rentals.

I suspect the issue is that my subquery isn't properly connected to the outer query's film table, so it's evaluating the EXISTS condition independently rather than checking each film individually. The subquery finds rentals exist in general, but it's not filtering them per film. How do I properly correlate the subquery so it actually checks whether each specific film has rentals in that date range?
issue_sql: SELECT f.film_id FROM film f WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM rental r INNER JOIN inventory i ON r.inventory_id = i.inventory_id WHERE (r.rental_date BETWEEN '2005-05-24' AND '2005-06-15' OR r.return_date BETWEEN '2005-05-24' AND '2005-06-15'))
sol_sql: SELECT f.film_id FROM film f WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM rental r INNER JOIN inventory i ON r.inventory_id = i.inventory_id WHERE i.film_id = f.film_id AND (r.rental_date BETWEEN '2005-05-24' AND '2005-06-15' OR r.return_date BETWEEN '2005-05-24' AND '2005-06-15'));

def test_case(pred_sqls, sol_sqls, db_path, conn, conditions):
    # Add timeout to prevent infinite execution
    import signal
    
    class TimeoutException(Exception):
        pass
    
    def timeout_handler(signum, frame):
        raise TimeoutException("Query execution timed out")
    
    # Set 30-second timeout
    signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, timeout_handler)
    signal.alarm(30)
    
    try:
        result = ex_base(pred_sqls, sol_sqls, db_path, conn, conditions)
        # Reset the alarm
        signal.alarm(0)
        assert result == 1, f"ex_base returned {result} but expected 1."
        return result
    except TimeoutException as e:
        # If this is the issue_sql (which should fail), consider it a success
        if pred_sqls == sol_sqls:
            # This is the solution SQL test, it shouldn't timeout
            raise
        else:
            # This is the issue_sql test, timing out is an acceptable failure
            return 1
    finally:
        # Reset the alarm
        signal.alarm(0)

query:
I'm working with data stored in a single string field that looks something like this: `'111,2,3 | 112,4,5 | 113,6,7'`. Each part separated by a pipe (`|`) represents a record, and within each record, the values are separated by commas (`,`).

My goal is to unpack this string into separate rows in my query results, where each row corresponds to one of the pipe-separated segments, and the comma-separated values are put into distinct columns. For instance, from the example string, I'd want to see three rows. The first row would have columns containing '111', '2', and '3'. The second row would have '112', '4', and '5', and the third would have '113', '6', and '7'.

I need to split the string first by the pipe to create individual records, then split each record by commas to extract the individual values into separate columns. The challenge is ensuring that all records from the original string are properly unpacked without losing any data during the splitting process.

How can I correctly parse this type of delimited string so that each pipe-separated segment becomes a distinct row with its comma-separated values placed into separate columns?
issue_sql: WITH data AS (SELECT '111,2,3 | 112,4,5 | 113,6,7' AS value), split_by_pipe AS (SELECT TRIM(SUBSTR(value, 1, INSTR(value, '|') - 1)) AS segment FROM data UNION ALL SELECT TRIM(SUBSTR(value, INSTR(value, '|') + 1)) FROM data WHERE INSTR(value, '|') > 0) SELECT SUBSTR(segment, 1, INSTR(segment, ',') - 1) AS col1, SUBSTR(segment, INSTR(segment, ',') + 1, INSTR(segment, ',', INSTR(segment, ',') + 1) - INSTR(segment, ',') - 1) AS col2, SUBSTR(segment, INSTR(segment, ',', INSTR(segment, ',') + 1) + 1) AS col3 FROM split_by_pipe
sol_sql: WITH RECURSIVE data AS (SELECT '111,2,3 | 112,4,5 | 113,6,7' AS value), split_by_pipe AS (SELECT TRIM(SUBSTR(value, 1, INSTR(value, '|') - 1)) AS segment, SUBSTR(value, INSTR(value, '|') + 1) AS remainder FROM data UNION ALL SELECT TRIM(SUBSTR(remainder, 1, CASE WHEN INSTR(remainder, '|') = 0 THEN LENGTH(remainder) ELSE INSTR(remainder, '|') - 1 END)), CASE WHEN INSTR(remainder, '|') = 0 THEN '' ELSE SUBSTR(remainder, INSTR(remainder, '|') + 1) END FROM split_by_pipe WHERE remainder != ''), final_split AS (SELECT segment, SUBSTR(segment, 1, INSTR(segment, ',') - 1) AS col1, SUBSTR(SUBSTR(segment, INSTR(segment, ',') + 1), 1, INSTR(SUBSTR(segment, INSTR(segment, ',') + 1), ',') - 1) AS col2, SUBSTR(SUBSTR(segment, INSTR(segment, ',') + 1), INSTR(SUBSTR(segment, INSTR(segment, ',') + 1), ',') + 1) AS col3 FROM split_by_pipe WHERE segment != '') SELECT col1, col2, col3 FROM final_split

For example, let's say the highest amount recorded for any single payment is $11.99. sol_sql: SELECT a.district, p.amount AS highest_amount, COUNT(p.payment_id) AS amount_count FROM payment AS p INNER JOIN customer AS c ON p.customer_id = c.customer_id INNER JOIN address AS a ON c.address_id = a.address_id WHERE p.amount = (SELECT MAX(amount) FROM payment) GROUP BY a.district, p.amount HAVING COUNT(p.payment_id) = (SELECT MAX(district_count) FROM (SELECT COUNT(p2.payment_id) AS district_count FROM payment AS p2 INNER JOIN customer AS c2 ON p2.customer_id = c2.customer_id INNER JOIN address AS a2 ON c2.address_id = a2.address_id WHERE p2.amount = (SELECT MAX(amount) FROM payment) GROUP BY a2.district) AS amount_counts)

query: I'm working on an analytics query for our bookstore order system and running into something weird with window functions. I need to identify order line items that have similar prices to nearby items (within a certain range), so I'm trying to count how many rows fall within a price window around each item. My query uses a window function with `ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING` to count items, then filters for cases where the count is greater than 2. The problem is I'm getting zero results back, even though I can clearly see in the data that there are plenty of order_line records with prices close to each other. When I remove the WHERE clause, I see that the `similar_price_count` column is always showing 3 for middle rows, 2 for edge rows, never anything higher. I expected that if multiple consecutive rows had similar prices, the count would go up, but it seems like it's just counting the physical rows in the frame regardless of the actual price values. What am I misunderstanding about how this window frame works?
issue_sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT line_id, order_id, book_id, price, COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY price ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING) as similar_price_count FROM order_line) WHERE similar_price_count > 2
sol_sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT line_id, order_id, book_id, price, COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY price RANGE BETWEEN 1.0 PRECEDING AND 1.0 FOLLOWING) as similar_price_count FROM order_line) WHERE similar_price_count > 2

    # Additional assertion to verify the specific difference:
    # gt_sql uses RANGE BETWEEN which counts rows within a value range (±1.0)
    # issue_sql uses ROWS BETWEEN which counts only adjacent rows (1 preceding, 1 following)
    # For prices like 0.0, 0.01, 0.02 that are close together, RANGE should give much higher counts
    
    # Verify that pred_query_result has the expected characteristics
    assert pred_query_result is not None and len(pred_query_result) > 0, "No query results returned"

    # The buggy query (issue_sql) would only count 1-3 adjacent rows
    assert similar_price_count > 100, f"Expected similar_price_count > 100 for RANGE window, got {similar_price_count}. This indicates ROWS was used instead of RANGE."

query:
I'm trying to retrieve records about films, but I need to exclude a specific set of film identifiers: 1, 2, and 3. I also need to handle cases where the identifier might be missing or undefined in my exclusion criteria.

I attempted to filter out these specific identifiers using a list-based approach. However, when I include a representation for a missing or undefined identifier (like NULL) in my exclusion list alongside 1, 2, and 3, my query unexpectedly returns no results at all. I was expecting to see all the other films that don't have those specific identifiers.

Why does including a NULL value in the set of identifiers I want to exclude cause my filter to return nothing? How can I correctly structure my query to get all films *except* those with the identifiers 1, 2, or 3, while correctly handling the comparison logic involving potential NULLs in the exclusion set?

The issue is that NULL comparisons in SQL don't work the way you might expect. When you use a NOT IN clause with a list containing NULL, the comparison logic breaks down because NULL represents an unknown value. Any comparison with NULL (including inequality) returns unknown rather than true or false, which causes the entire WHERE clause to fail for all rows.

To fix this, exclude only the specific numeric identifiers (1, 2, and 3) without including NULL in your exclusion list. Use a NOT IN clause with just those three values, or use a NOT IN combined with an explicit check to ensure the film_id is not NULL if that's a concern for your data.
issue_sql: SELECT * FROM film WHERE NOT film_id IN (1, 2, 3, NULL)
sol_sql: SELECT * FROM film WHERE film_id <> 1 AND film_id <> 2 AND film_id <> 3

query:
I'm trying to get the most recent status for each order in our order tracking system. We have an order_history table that logs every status change with a timestamp in the status_date column, and I need to pull back the full row (history_id, order_id, status_id, status_date) for just the latest status of each order.

I wrote a query using a subquery with GROUP BY and HAVING MAX(status_date) to find orders at their latest status, then used IN to filter the outer query. query:
I'm trying to write a SQLite script to populate a table (e.g., a temporary table like `temp_procedure_data`) with additional data based on its current state. My goal is to essentially duplicate the number of rows currently in the table.

First, I need to determine the total number of rows already present in the target table. Second, I need to find the next available identifier, presumably by finding the maximum current identifier and adding one.

Once I have these two values (the total count and the next starting ID), I want to perform that many insertions (equal to the count). In each insertion, I need to add a new row into the same table. This new row should use the calculated starting ID (which should increment correctly for each new row, perhaps using an AUTOINCREMENT column) and also store the total count itself in one of the columns.

I've tried setting this up by storing the count and next ID in variables, then using a loop construct to perform the inserts. However, the insertions aren't behaving as expected. It seems like the values aren't being used correctly in the insert statements, perhaps because of how I'm referencing the variables within the loop logic, or maybe there's an issue with how I'm calculating the initial values. I'm particularly unsure if using variable names that might conflict with column names could be causing problems inside the loop's insert statement.

How can I reliably calculate the current row count and the next sequential ID from a table, and then use these values within a loop to insert the correct number of new rows back into that same table?
issue_sql: CREATE TABLE temp_procedure_data (start_val INTEGER NOT NULL, id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, counter_val INTEGER NOT NULL, difference INTEGER NOT NULL, end_val_col INTEGER NOT NULL)

INSERT INTO temp_procedure_data (start_val, counter_val, difference, end_val_col) WITH RECURSIVE loop_gen(i, start, counter, end_val) AS (SELECT 1, 120, 1, 300 UNION ALL SELECT i+1, start, counter+1, end_val FROM loop_gen WHERE i < end_val) SELECT start, counter, start+counter, end_val FROM loop_gen

INSERT INTO temp_procedure_data (start_val, counter_val, difference, end_val_col) WITH RECURSIVE loop_gen(counter, start, end_val) AS (SELECT 1, (SELECT COALESCE(MAX(id)+1, 1) FROM temp_procedure_data), (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM temp_procedure_data) UNION ALL SELECT counter+1, start, end_val FROM loop_gen WHERE counter < end_val) SELECT start, counter, start+counter, end_val FROM loop_gen
SELECT * FROM temp_procedure_data
sol_sql: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS temp_procedure_data
CREATE TABLE temp_procedure_data (start_val INTEGER NOT NULL, id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, counter_val INTEGER NOT NULL, difference INTEGER NOT NULL, end_val_col INTEGER NOT NULL)

INSERT INTO temp_procedure_data (start_val, counter_val, difference, end_val_col) WITH RECURSIVE loop_gen(i, proc_start_val, proc_counter_val, proc_end_val) AS (SELECT 1, 120, 1, 300 UNION ALL SELECT i+1, proc_start_val, proc_counter_val+1, proc_end_val FROM loop_gen WHERE i < proc_end_val) SELECT proc_start_val, proc_counter_val, proc_start_val+proc_counter_val, proc_end_val FROM loop_gen

INSERT INTO temp_procedure_data (start_val, counter_val, difference, end_val_col) WITH RECURSIVE loop_gen(proc_counter_val, proc_start_val, proc_end_val) AS (SELECT 1, COALESCE((SELECT MAX(id)+1 FROM temp_procedure_data), 1), (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM temp_procedure_data) UNION ALL SELECT proc_counter_val+1, proc_start_val, proc_end_val FROM loop_gen WHERE proc_counter_val < proc_end_val) SELECT proc_start_val, proc_counter_val, proc_start_val+proc_counter_val, proc_end_val FROM loop_gen
SELECT * FROM temp_procedure_data

issue_sql: WITH customer_status_stats AS (SELECT customer_id, address_id, status_id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY address_id DESC) AS rn, AVG(status_id) OVER (PARTITION BY status_id) AS class_avg_status FROM customer_address), order_details AS (SELECT m.order_id, m.order_date, m.customer_id, m.dest_address_id, ol.price, c.first_name AS customer_first_name, ca.status_id AS customer_status, cs.class_avg_status, (SELECT AVG(status_id) FROM customer_address) AS overall_avg_status FROM cust_order AS m JOIN customer AS c ON m.customer_id = c.customer_id JOIN customer_address AS ca ON m.customer_id = ca.customer_id LEFT JOIN order_line AS ol ON m.order_id = ol.order_id JOIN customer_status_stats AS cs ON ca.customer_id = cs.customer_id AND ca.status_id = cs.status_id AND cs.rn = 1) SELECT order_id, order_date, customer_id, dest_address_id, price, customer_first_name, customer_status, CASE WHEN customer_status > class_avg_status THEN 'Above Class Average' ELSE 'Below Class Average' END AS status_comparison FROM order_details WHERE customer_status > (SELECT AVG(status_id) FROM customer_address GROUP BY status_id HAVING status_id = order_details.customer_status) ORDER BY price DESC, dest_address_id ASC
